title: Chapter 4: The Confession Booth
content: The bar smells like stale beer and regret, which is redundant because this whole place is built on those two things. Frankie's. No apostrophe, no pretense. Just a handwritten sign taped to the window that says COLD BEER in letters that have been bleeding down the glass since before I was born. The floor sticks to my elbows where I'm leaning on the table — someone spilled something here and nobody bothered to clean it properly, and I'm choosing to believe it's soda because the alternative will make me leave, and I can't leave yet because leaving means going home and going home means trying to sleep and trying to sleep means closing my eyes and closing my eyes means—
A loaded fry lands in the puddle of ketchup on Mika's side of the table.
"You're not eating," she says.
"I'm not hungry."

"You haven't been hungry all week." She picks up the fry, examines it, eats it anyway. "You also haven't been sleeping. Or answering my texts with anything longer than 'fine.' And you're wearing the same blazer you wore Monday, which means you're not going home to change, which means you're either working yourself to death or you've started living at the office, and those are the same thing."

The neon beer sign above the bar buzzes pink and blue and pink again, casting Mika's purple hair in shifting light. She's watching me with that expression — the one where her eyebrows do this almost-flat thing and her mouth goes soft and serious, and I know she's cataloging every detail, building a case. Mika's a paralegal at a mid-size firm in Midtown, and she approaches everything like cross-examination. She's been my best friend since sophomore year, when I crashed into her in the library and knocked her iced coffee all over her Converse and she looked at me and said, "You owe me shoes and a study partner, in that order."
I owe her a lot more than shoes now.
"I'm fine," I say.
A fry hits my hand. She threw it.
"Aves."
"Mika."

"Don't 'Mika' me." She leans back in the booth, arms crossed. The vinyl squeaks under her. "You got the Goldman job. Sterling Capital, whatever — you got the prestigious Wall Street thing that you've been working toward since I met you. You should be celebrating. Buying rounds. Doing shots of the cheap tequila that gives you the hiccups." She pauses. "Instead you look like you're attending your own funeral."
I try on a smile. "Maybe I'm just pre-grieving Wall Street."

The joke lands like a brick in a swimming pool. Mika doesn't laugh. She sets down the fry she was holding — sets it down carefully, deliberately, the way you set down something when you need both hands free — and looks at me.
"Talk to me."

The thing about Mika is that she never raises her voice when it matters. She goes quieter. Softer. Like she's making space for whatever ugly thing needs to come out, and she doesn't want to spook it. It's the same voice she used when I called her at 2 AM junior year, crying about my mom's diagnosis. The same voice she used when our friend Dean got his DUI. The same voice she uses for things that are actually, irreversibly real.
I open my mouth.
Close it.

What can I possibly say? I've been living three lives and one of them is in Paris and I think I'm losing my mind? I found an app on my phone that shouldn't exist and it shows me alternate timelines like a menu at a restaurant I never walked into? Every time I switch between my Wall Street life and my gallery-in-Paris life, something tears a little more, and last Tuesday I got a nosebleed so bad I bled through three tissues at my desk and Hadley asked if I was okay and I said seasonal allergies in February?
Yeah. No. That's not dinner conversation.
So instead I say the only part of it that sounds sane.

"I feel like I'm living the wrong life." The words come out quieter than I intend. "Like somewhere out there, there's a version of me who made all the right choices, and she's happy. And I'm just... the draft. The rough copy."
Mika stares at me. The neon sign cycles pink. Her hair glows like a bruise.
"That's the most privileged thing you've ever said." Her voice is flat. Not angry — measured. "You KNOW that, right?"
The words hit like a slap. I actually flinch.
"Excuse me?"

"Avery." She leans forward, elbows on the sticky table, and she doesn't even seem to notice the tackiness under her forearms. "You have a job most people would kill for. You graduated top of our class. You're healthy — don't give me that face, you ARE — you're brilliant, you have people who love you, and you're sitting here in a bar telling me you feel like you're in the wrong life because you can't handle the fact that choosing something means NOT choosing something else?"
My throat tightens. "That's not what I—"

"That's exactly what you said." She doesn't let me escape. "You said there's a version of you who made 'all the right choices' and she's happy. Implying you made wrong choices. Implying you're not happy. Implying the problem is the choices instead of, I don't know, the fact that you've never once in your life had to face a consequence you couldn't study your way out of."
"Mika—"

"I love you. You know I love you." She says it like a knife — sharp, precise, unavoidable. "But you're doing that thing you do where you imagine a problem that's not there because actually sitting with the life you have and being okay with it terrifies you. Because if you're okay, then there's nothing left to chase. And you don't know who you are without something to chase."

The bar noise fills the space between us. Someone feeds coins into the old jukebox. A man at the bar argues with the bartender about the Yankees. Two women in the corner laugh at something on a phone. Normal sounds. Normal people. Normal lives.

And I'm sitting here with my heart slamming against my ribs and my eyes burning because she's right, and I've never been called on it before, and I didn't know it would feel like this — like being skinned, like being seen so clearly that the air hurts.
"I know," I whisper.

Because here's the thing Mika doesn't know, the thing I can't tell her, the thing I'm barely holding together: she's right about the privilege, she's right about the chase, but she's wrong about one critical thing. The problem IS real. The other lives aren't hypotheticals I'm romanticizing. They're not what-ifs I'm daydreaming about to avoid the present. They're there. On my phone. Living without me. Or — worse — living because of me. Because I'm living them.

Paris-Avery brushed paint on a canvas yesterday. She thinks about Julian Vance — the same Julian Vance who sat across from me at Sterling Capital, whose face I recognized from a gallery I've never been to — and she's starting to feel something she can't name. I can feel it when I switch back: the residue of her emotions clinging to mine like perfume I didn't ask to wear.

Wall Street-Avery is performing. Surviving. She's good at it — terrifyingly good — but she's not real. She's the version of me who chose the path everyone expected, and she's hollow in the way a house is hollow when no one's home.

And then there's the third path. The one I haven't opened. The one marked with a question mark on the Fork app. It sits on the screen like a door I'm afraid to knock on because whatever's behind it might be the version of me who made the choice I can't even admit I want.
I need to tell Mika something. Anything. The silence is turning into a wall.

"There's this... app." I hear myself say it before I've decided to. "On my phone. I didn't download it. It showed up after graduation night and it — it shows me things. Other paths. Other lives. And I think..." I swallow. "I think they might be real."

I expect her to laugh. I expect the Mika who handles real-world problems — bad landlords, messed-up paychecks, leaky faucets — to look at me like I've lost my mind. I prepare for the gentle "Aves, you need therapy" speech. I deserve it. I probably do need therapy.
Instead she reaches across the table.
"Give me your phone."
"Mika—"
"Give. Me. Your. Phone."
I dig it out of my pocket. The screen is dark. I hand it over, and my hand is shaking, and she notices but doesn't comment.

She presses the power button. The lock screen glows — a photo of us at Coney Island, sunburned and stupid and happy. She enters my passcode (it's her birthday; she knows this). She swipes through my home screens.
And she finds it.

The black icon. No label. No identifier. Just a shape like a fork in a road, rendered in negative space against the phone's dark background, almost invisible unless you know it's there. Mika stares at it for a long time. The neon sign buzzes above us. Pink light catches the edge of the screen.
"Show me."
Her voice is quiet. Not doubtful. Not mocking. Quiet the way she gets when she's scared but refusing to show it.
I take the phone back. Open the app.

The three paths bloom on the screen. PATH A: The white curve of an office building. Wall Street. The path I'm on — the one I chose, the one that's supposed to be enough. PATH B: A gallery wall with a single painting. Paris. The path that chose me. PATH C: The question mark. The unknown. Below each icon, status indicators pulse faintly — little green lights like I'm monitoring three servers instead of three lives.

Mika's face is unreadable. She's leaning in, close enough that I can smell the fry grease on her fingers and the vanilla of her lotion underneath. Her eyes move across the screen: left, right, left again. She's processing. I've watched her process depositions; she reads them like maps, finding the contradictions, the fault lines. Right now she's mapping something that shouldn't exist, and I can see her brain refusing to reject it, filing it under "unexplained but not impossible."
She reaches out and takes the phone from my hands. Gently. Like it might bite her. She stares at PATH B for a long time.
"This one," she says. "What is this one?"
"Paris."
"You've been to Paris?"
"I—" The word catches. "Yes. But also no."

She looks at me. Really looks at me. And for the first time tonight, I don't think she's seeing the privileged girl who's chasing something she shouldn't need. I think she's seeing something she can't explain, and it's scaring her, and she's not running.
She hands the phone back.

"Aves." Her voice is barely above the jukebox, barely above the argument at the bar, barely above the sound of my own pulse in my ears. "If this is real — and I'm not saying it is — but IF it is..." She swallows. Her hand finds mine on the sticky table. Her fingers are warm. They're shaking. "You know you can't live in three places at once, right?"
The question hangs between us like smoke. Like the condensation on the beer I haven't touched. Like the silence in a confession booth after you've said the thing you can't take back.

I should say yes. I should say I know. I should promise to delete the app, stay in one life, be the version of me who chose Sterling Capital and meant it. That's what she's asking for. That's what love sounds like — the kind that calls you out, the kind that holds your hand while it does.
But I don't answer.
I can't.

Because the truth is worse than anything Mika is imagining. The truth is that I already AM living in three places at once, and every time I switch, I feel myself tearing a little more, and I can't stop. I can't stop the way you can't stop breathing. I can't stop the way you can't stop dreaming. The app opened something in me that doesn't want to close, and the worst part — the part I'll never say out loud — is that I don't want it to close.
I want all three lives. I want the wrongness of wanting them. I want the impossibility. Because for the first time since I can remember, I don't feel like I'm missing something.
I feel like I'm everything.
And that's the problem.

Mika waits. The jukebox switches songs. The neon sign buzzes pink and blue and pink. My beer sweats onto the table. My nose itches — the precursor, the warning, the thing that means blood is coming, and I pinch the bridge of my nose and pray it holds for another five minutes.
The silence stretches.

It holds all of it — the privilege and the truth and the fear and the love and the impossibility of choosing just one life when you've touched the edges of more. Mika's hand tightens around mine. She's waiting for an answer I can't give, and the not-giving is its own answer, and we both know it.

This is what FOMO really is. Not missing out on the party. Not scrolling Instagram and wishing you were somewhere else. It's sitting across from your best friend in a dive bar that smells like stale beer and bad decisions, and she's asking you to choose — just choose, Aves, choose one — and you can't.
Not because you don't know which one you want.
Because you want them all.
